CONCLUS ION
As an efected sacramento city Councilmenber you are a public
official, 
and as such, you are prohibj.ted from making,
participating in making or in any way attenpting to use your
orficial 
position to influence a governmental- decision which will-
foreseeabl,y have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally, on your economic interests.
FACTS
You are the Sacramento City councilmember for District one of
the city. 
You stated that you co-own a duplex in the Land Park
area of Sacramento and are purchasing a parcel with your spouse
for his business to use as a shop and storage area. You have
requested guidance with respect to several decisions and their
po€entiat effect on your real property interests.
First you have asked about a future decision to form a
redevelopment area which would include property that you are
acquirj,n!. Additj-onaIlY, You have asked about a proposed artists'
Iive/wori space which would be two blocks frorn your real property.
You irave alio asked about two other decisions concerning proposed
sites one block from your property: (l') a major road improvement
plan, the Arden-Garden Connector, and, (2) decisions concerning
the city's efforts to encouraqe retai-] and business users to
occupy spaces along Det Paso Boulevard. FinaIly, you have asked
qeneiaffy about potential, conflicts of interest with respect to
your i.nterest in a duplex in the Land Park area of sacranento'
ANALYSI S
The Act hras enacted by the people of the State of California
by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the conf I ict-of- interest
piovisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, 
$'hether
ilected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial
nanner, free- from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported then'
(section 810o1(b) . )
In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
from making, participating in
rnaking, or oLherwise using his or her official- position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial inierest. 
A 'tpublic official" 
is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person vho is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. This definition incLudes elected city
counci Imembers .


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-089
Page 3
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wiII have a naterial financial effect,
distinguishable frorn the effect on ttre public generally, on the
officiit 
or a member of his or her irnmediate family or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($f-,OOO) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the Public-
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,o00) or more'
(c) Any source of incorne, other than gifts
and other than l-oans by a commercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms avail-able to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or nore in vatue provided to,
received'by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tine when the
decision is nade.
Section 871o3 (a) - (c) .

interests.

income to you. Moreover, if you own a home and reside at a

interest.

consequently you may not participate in any decisiori if ii i=

1. Real Propertv
A. The Redevelopnent Decision
whether the financiaf consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particulir 
case. An effect is
considered reasonabry foreseeibre if there is a substantiii
likelihood that it wiIl occur. Certainty is not ..q"i."Jl-
However, if an effect is only a mere po=iiUifity, 
i€ i= not
reasonably foreseeable.. (fn re Thorner (Lg75) i'f,ppc ops. 198.)
since the purpose of the r"dev"-oprnEnt-pian is to pi"*"-t"'=ouna

affected.

..

the property is directl-y involved in the decisiln or indiiectly
invorved. . (Regulation r.8702. ) rf the decision a i.."iiy -"it""t=

property_ interests, 
d isqua 1if ication i=-i.q"i."a.
(Regulation 18702.1.) An official,s 
r6aI property inteieJi ls
directly involved in a decision if:
?he decision. is to designate the survey area,
to select the project area, to adopt the

official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
focated within the boundarj-es (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
Regulation LA702.1(a) (3) (D) .
Thus, the decision to establish a redevelopnent area tfrat
encompasses your real property is deerned to have a naterial
finantial eflect on yoirr proplrty and you may not participate.{
Ho$rever, public officials with real property interests that
will be financially affected by a governmental decision rnay
participate if the effect on their property is not distinguishabl-e
from the effect on the public generally. For the 'rpubJ-ic
generallytt exception to app1y, a decisj.on must affect the
official's 
interests in substantially the same manner as it would
affect a significant segment of the public in the official-'s
jurisdiction. 
(Regulation 187o3 . )
For exanple, in In re or^/en (197 6) 2 FPPC Ops. 77 , t;l-e
Comrniss.ion deterrnined that owners of residential property in and
irnmediately adjacent to the rrcore arearr in the city of Davis v/ere
a significant segment of the jurisdiction 
and would be sirnilarly
affected by various land use decisions within the core area.
consequentfy, a planning commissioner who osrned a residence
irnmediately adjacent to the core area could participate in the
decisions provided there was no evidence that the effect on the
comnissj,oner's property would be different than the effect on
other owners of residentiat property in the area.
Thus, for the t'public generally" exception to appl,y to your
situation, the redevel-opnent area in question would need to
encotnpass a signif j.cant segTment of Sacramento and affect the
population in the area in substantj,ally the same manner as it
would affect your property interests. 
we do not have sufficient
facts at this time to deterrnine if the I'pubLic generally'l
exception would apply to your situation. 
The test is necessarily _
a fact-dependent test and its application would depend on the size
of the redeveloprnent area and the type of decision in question.
You should contact us once the issue reaches a more concrete
stage .

Regulation L87o2.3 provides guidelines as to whether the
financial effect on a real property interest indirectly affected
by a decision is material.
(a) The effect of a decision is materiaL as
to real property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
foltowing appJ- ies:
(1) The real property in which the
official 
has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located wi,thin a 30o foot
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
wilI have no financial effect upon the
official's 
real property j.nterest.
(2) The decision involves construction
of, or irnprovements to, streets, water, sewer,
storrn drainage or similar facilities, 
and the
real property in which the official 
has an
interest will receive ner., or substantially
irnproved services.
(3) The real property in which the
official 
has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the rea]
property is located within a radius of 2,5oo
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundarj-es) of the property which is the
subject of the decisj.on and the decision wj-1I
have a reasonably foreseeable financial- effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the offj.cial has
an interest; or

the property by 9l-,O0o or more pet 72

Thus, for example, if we assume you are referring to a

3oo feet, yoir would be required to disqualify frorn the decisions
if there would be a foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or

irateriality to you and your city attorney within the guidelines
provided by Regulation 18702.3. )
2. Your Spouse's Business
Even if your property interest is not disqualifying !'ith
respect to a partitullr 
decision, you rnay sti11 be required to
aisqualify yoirrself from participating . in the decision if the

n.ve a foreseeable material financial effect on your
spouse's business.
The standard for foreseeability is set forth above' Your
questions concern indirect effects on your spouse's business,
s'ince the business v/as not the applicant for any of the decisions.
whether the indirect effect on a business entity is rnaterial
depends on the financial size of the business entity' 
F9'
ex^anple, Regulation La702.2 (g) provides that for a relatively
smali busin6ss entity, the indirect effect of a decision is
material- where:
(1) The decis.ion witl resul't in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurrinq or avoidinq additionaf expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of 92,500 or more; or
(3) The decision wil,I result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or l-iabifities
of $10,000 or more.

FACTS You are the Sacramento City councilmember for District one of the city. You stated that you co-own a duplex in the Land Park area of Sacramento and are purchasing a parcel with your spouse for his business to use as a shop and storage area. You have requested guidance with respect to several decisions and their po€entiat effect on your real property interests. First you have asked about a future decision to form a redevelopment area which would include property that you are acquirj,n!.
